abstract: This research explores the strategies employed by Islamic Religious Education (PAI) teachers at SDN 2 Kesumadadi to cultivate religious character in students through the habituation of reciting the Asma’ al-Husna (the 99 Names of Allah). While the formal curriculum for this level typically covers only four specific names, the PAI teachers introduce the complete set of names to foster deeper spiritual roots and overcome student anxieties regarding Arabic literacy. Using a qualitative field research approach, data were gathered via observation, in-depth interviews, and documentation. The findings reveal that while the collective recitation is integrated into religious lessons rather than a daily morning routine, it serves as a vital pedagogical tool. The integration strategy relies on five key pillars: providing exemplary leadership (uswah hasanah), consistent habituation, proactive mentoring—including the use of Latin transliterations for students struggling with Arabic script—positive reinforcement through appreciation, and the enforcement of discipline. Ultimately, these efforts aim to internalize religious values and enhance the spiritual and emotional intelligence of students within the elementary school environment.
